title: Flush Draw on the Flop — Calling a Half-Pot Bet
actionHistory: You raised to 25 preflop from CO, BB called. Flop is Kh-7h-2s. BB bets 30.

options.optionExplanation: Folding a nut flush draw with excellent pot odds is far too tight.
You need 25% equity to call, and your flush draw gives you about 35%. Easy call.
Raising as a semi-bluff is viable but calling is more profitable here since you have direct odds.

explanation: You have the nut flush draw with 9 outs. The pot is 60 and BB bets 30, making the pot 90. You need to call 30 to win 90, giving you pot odds of 30/(90+30) = 25%. Your flush draw has roughly 35% equity (9 outs × ~4% on the flop with two cards to come using the rule of 4). Since 35% > 25%, you have a clear profitable call. You don't need to raise because your hand has enough equity to call directly — save the aggression for when you don't have direct odds.
keyConceptSummary: When your drawing equity exceeds the pot odds required to call, calling is automatically profitable.

title: Gutshot on the Turn — Overbet from Villain
actionHistory: MP raised preflop, you called on BTN. Flop 9c-4h-2d, MP bet 20, you called. Turn Ks, MP bets 100.

options.optionExplanation: With only a gutshot (4 outs, ~8% equity) facing a pot-sized overbet, you're nowhere near the odds to call.
You need ~36% equity to call this sizing but only have about 8%. Burning money.
Bluff-raising the turn into a player showing massive strength is very risky with no fold equity.

explanation: You have a gutshot straight draw needing a Queen (4 outs). On the turn with one card to come, use the rule of 2: 4 × 2 = 8% equity. The pot is 80 and villain bets 100, so you need to call 100 into a pot of 180. That's 100/280 = 36% equity needed. Your 8% is nowhere close to 36%. This is a clear fold. Many beginners chase gutshots because 'a straight would be so nice,' but the math says you lose money calling here roughly 4 out of 5 times you don't improve.
keyConceptSummary: On the turn, use the rule of 2 (outs × 2) for equity. Gutshots with 4 outs rarely have the odds to call big bets.
villainRange: MP strong range: AA, KK, AK, KQ, 99, sets

title: Open-Ended Straight Draw — Small Bet on Flop
actionHistory: CO raised to 25, you called from BB. Flop 9d-6c-2h. CO bets 15.

options.optionExplanation: Folding an OESD getting amazing odds is a huge mistake.
You have 8 outs (~32% with two cards to come) and only need ~19% equity. Slam dunk call.
Check-raising as a semi-bluff is reasonable but calling is simpler and clearly profitable.

explanation: You have an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or T makes a straight — 8 outs). CO bets 15 into a pot of 50. You need to call 15 to win 65, giving you pot odds of 15/80 = 19%. Your OESD has roughly 32% equity on the flop (8 outs × 4 using the rule of 4). Since 32% far exceeds 19%, this is a very profitable call. The small bet sizing from CO gives you an incredible price. Even a check-raise as a semi-bluff has merit, but the math makes calling trivially profitable here.
keyConceptSummary: Open-ended straight draws have 8 outs (~32% on the flop). Against small bets, you almost always have the odds to continue.
villainRange: CO opening range: overpairs, top pair, AK, AQ, broadways

title: Combo Draw — Flush Draw Plus Gutshot
actionHistory: CO raised to 25, you called BTN. BB called. Flop Jh-3h-7c. CO bets 50. BB folds. Action on you.

options.optionExplanation: Folding a 12-out combo draw with two cards to come is way too weak.
With ~45% equity and needing ~29%, calling is very profitable.
Semi-bluff raising is excellent — you have massive equity and fold equity combined.

explanation: You have a monster combo draw: flush draw (9 outs) plus a gutshot to a straight with an 8 (3 additional outs, since 8h is already counted in flush outs). That's 12 outs, giving you roughly 45% equity on the flop (12 × 4 = 48%, slightly adjusted). The pot is 70, CO bets 50, so calling requires 50/170 = 29% equity. You easily have the odds to call. But raising is even better here — you have so much equity that a semi-bluff raise puts tremendous pressure on CO. If they fold, you win immediately. If they call, you still have nearly a coin flip. This is the dream semi-bluff scenario.
keyConceptSummary: Combo draws (flush draw + straight draw) are semi-bluff powerhouses — you can raise aggressively because you have massive equity as backup.
villainRange: CO c-bet range: overpairs, top pair, AK, AQ, some bluffs

title: Implied Odds — Set Mining Preflop
actionHistory: UTG raises to 25. Action on you in MP.

options.optionExplanation: Set mining with deep stacks and good implied odds. You need to hit a set ~1 in 8 times and can win a big pot when you do.
Folding is safe but too tight. With nearly 100BB stacks, the implied odds justify a call.
3-betting 55 from MP against a UTG raise is terrible. You're turning a speculative hand into a bluff against a strong range.

explanation: Pocket fives don't have great direct pot odds to call — you'll flop a set about 12% of the time (roughly 1 in 8). But with nearly 100BB effective stacks, implied odds make this profitable. The concept: you invest 25 to potentially win a much larger pot when you hit your set. If UTG has AA/KK/AK and you flop a set, they'll often put in 100+ more chips. The rule of thumb for set mining is you need roughly 15-20× the call amount behind in effective stacks. Here that's 15 × 25 = 375, and you have 975 behind — plenty of implied odds. Fold if stacks were short (under 300).
keyConceptSummary: Implied odds justify calling with small pairs when stacks are deep enough. You need about 15-20× the call amount in remaining stacks.
villainRange: UTG open: TT+, AJs+, AQo+, KQs

title: River Call — Pot Odds vs Bluff Frequency
actionHistory: BTN raised preflop, you called BB. You checked every street. BTN bet flop, bet turn, now bets 80 on river.

options.optionExplanation: You need to be right 29% of the time. Top pair is ahead of enough bluffs in villain's range.
Folding top pair on a dry board where villain could easily be bluffing is too weak.
Raising for value with one pair is very risky — you only get called by hands that beat you.

explanation: On the river, pot odds become about bluff-catching math. The pot is 120, villain bets 80, so you need to call 80 to win 200. That's 80/280 = 29% equity needed. The question becomes: is villain bluffing more than 29% of the time? On this dry A-8-4-3-7 board, villain's triple-barrel range includes value hands (AK, AQ, sets, two pair) but also busted draws and pure bluffs. With top pair decent kicker on a board where no draws completed, villain has plenty of bluffs in their range. You only need to be right 29% of the time — this is a call.
keyConceptSummary: On the river, pot odds tell you how often you need to be right to call. Against a 2/3 pot bet, you need to win just 29% of the time.
villainRange: BTN 3-barrel: AA, AK, AQ, 88, 44, plus bluffs (KQs, QJs, missed draws)

title: Overcalling — Third Player in the Pot
actionHistory: CO raised to 25, you called BTN, BB called. Flop Th-5h-2c. BB bets 50, CO calls 50. Action on you.

options.optionExplanation: Incredible pot odds with a flush draw plus two overcards. You're calling 50 into 175 needing ~22% equity with ~45% equity.
Folding this monster draw at these odds is a massive mistake.
Raising into two opponents who have shown strength is risky. Calling is safer and still very profitable.

explanation: You have a flush draw (9 outs) plus two overcards that could make top pair (potentially 6 more outs, though discounted). That's roughly 12-15 outs, giving you ~45% equity on the flop. The pot is 75 + 50 + 50 = 175, and you need to call 50. Your pot odds are 50/225 = 22%. With 45% equity versus 22% needed, this is an incredibly profitable call. In multiway pots, you often get better odds because other players sweeten the pot. This is a textbook example: the more players who put money in before you act, the better your price to draw.
keyConceptSummary: Multiway pots give you better pot odds because other players add money before you. Big draws become even more profitable to continue with.
villainRange: BB donk + CO call: top pair, sets, draws, two pair

title: Chasing Without Odds — Flush Draw Facing All-In
actionHistory: You raised preflop, BTN called. Flop Ad-7d-3c, you bet 30, BTN called. Turn Js. You check, BTN goes all-in for 250.

options.optionExplanation: You need 42% equity but only have about 20%. The all-in removes your implied odds too.
Calling 250 into 350 needs 42% equity. Your flush draw gives ~20% on the turn. This loses money long-term.

explanation: You have a flush draw on the turn with 9 outs. Using the rule of 2: 9 × 2 = 18% equity (actual is closer to 20%). Villain shoves 250 into 100, so you need to call 250 to win 350. That's 250/600 = 42% equity needed. Your 20% is less than half of what you need. This is a key lesson: on the turn, you only have one card to come, so draws are worth much less than on the flop. Also, an all-in means zero implied odds — you can't win any additional money on the river. Many beginners go broke chasing flush draws on the turn against large bets. The math says fold.
keyConceptSummary: On the turn, draws have roughly half the equity they had on the flop (rule of 2 vs rule of 4). Large bets require more equity than most draws provide.
villainRange: BTN all-in: two pair (AJ), sets, AK, AQ, possibly a bluff

title: Rule of 4 and 2 — Comparing Flop vs Turn Equity
actionHistory: You raised preflop from CO, BB called. Flop Tc-9s-3h. BB bets 45.

options.optionExplanation: OESD with two overcards. You have ~32% equity on the flop and need ~30%. Close but profitable, plus implied odds.
Folding an OESD with overcards getting close to correct odds is too tight.
Semi-bluff raising has merit but risks getting re-raised off your equity.

explanation: You have an open-ended straight draw (any K or 8 makes a straight — 8 outs) plus two overcards that might be good (Q and J). On the flop, use the rule of 4 for two cards to come: 8 outs × 4 = 32%. The pot is 60, BB bets 45, so you need 45/150 = 30% equity. Your 32% barely exceeds the 30% needed, making this a marginally profitable call. But here's the crucial detail: if you don't improve on the turn and face another bet, you'll use the rule of 2 instead: 8 × 2 = 16%. That's a huge drop. The rule of 4 assumes you see both remaining cards, but that's only guaranteed if you're all-in or facing no further bets. Plan for the possibility of facing another bet on the turn.
keyConceptSummary: Rule of 4 (flop, two cards to come) vs Rule of 2 (turn, one card to come). Be careful: rule of 4 equity only applies if you'll definitely see the river.
villainRange: BB donk bet: T9, T8, 99, 33, T7s, flush draws, pair + draw

title: Reverse Implied Odds — Dominated Flush Draw
actionHistory: UTG raised preflop, you called BB. Flop Ah-Kh-8c, UTG bet 25, you called. Turn 2s, UTG bets 35.

options.optionExplanation: Despite having a flush draw, your flush is small and a UTG raiser likely has bigger hearts. Reverse implied odds kill your profitability.
The pot odds look okay (35/160 = 22%, you have ~18%), but the bigger problem is when you hit — you may lose to a bigger flush.

explanation: This is a reverse implied odds trap. Yes, you have a flush draw with 9 outs (approximately 18% on the turn). The pot odds are 35/160 = 22%, which is close. But the critical issue is REVERSE implied odds: UTG raised from early position and c-bet an A-K board. Their range is loaded with big aces — and many of those include a heart (Ah is on the board, but AQh, AJh, ATh, KQh are all in their range). If you make your flush, there's a real chance UTG has a bigger flush and you lose your entire stack. Reverse implied odds mean that even when you hit your draw, you sometimes lose big. This turns a marginal call into a fold.
keyConceptSummary: Reverse implied odds occur when completing your draw might make you second-best. Small flush draws on boards where opponents likely hold bigger flush cards are dangerous.
villainRange: UTG range on AK board: AA, KK, AK, AQ, AJs+, KQs — many holding hearts
